topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien beneath my fingertips. I stared at the meticulously crafted narrative, the words flowing around my life like a river I hadn't chosen to swim in. Every anecdote, every carefully chosen descriptor, was a piece of me, yet it wasn't mine. A coldness settled deep in my chest, a heavy, suffocating weight that made my breath catch in my throat. I reread the passage about my childhood, the details so intimate, so perfectly rendered, and a tremor started in my hands. I pushed the document away, the sound of the paper rustling echoing loudly in the silence of the room. I needed air, desperately, but the air felt thin, incapable of reaching the knot tightening in my stomach.

[story 2]

"This is impossible," I whispered, the sound brittle and thin. My gaze darted back and forth across the pages, searching for any flaw, any misplaced comma that might betray the deception. The ink seemed to mock me—perfect, indelible, and utterly false. I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles bleached white. A strange, hot flush crept up my neck, spreading across my cheeks. I stood abruptly, pacing the small confines of the study, my movements jerky and agitated. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. Every logical pathway in my mind slammed shut, leaving only a churning, hollow feeling where certainty used to reside.

[story 3]

She slid the file across the mahogany surface. I didn't touch it immediately; instead, I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning under my sudden shift in weight. My eyes scanned the text, absorbing the sheer audacity of it. It wasn't just inaccurate; it was a complete fabrication of my existence. A slow, deliberate shake ran through my shoulders, a physical manifestation of the disorientation washing over me. I finally picked up the pen, not to correct, but just to hold it, feeling the weight of the betrayal settle into my bones. My voice, when I finally spoke, was dangerously low, laced with a tremor that betrayed the stillness of my exterior.
